region_of_origin: Taiwan; widely consumed in Singapore as a hawker food

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Tian Bu La; 'fish cake (food product)' FOODON_03315595 is the closest defensible FoodOn parent anchor. Tian Bu La is a deep-fried fish paste product with tapioca starch, distinct from plain fish cake.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fishery products — processed fish products
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers processed fish products broadly; no specific code exists for Tian Bu La or Taiwanese fish tempura within the FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Tian Bu La or equivalent Taiwanese fish tempura product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Tian Bu La is one piece weighing approximately 50 g, commonly consumed as a hawker snack or side dish in Singapore and Taiwan.

nutrient_source.title: Tian Bu La — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tian Bu La (F03060037). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Tapioca starch / tapioca-based products — analogous GI estimate from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tian Bu La in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 75 (high) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being tapioca starch, which has a published GI of approximately 70–85 in the University of Sydney GI Database for tapioca and tapioca-based products. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 35.4 × 0.5) / 100 = 13.3 for a 50 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 944 mg per 100 g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Tian Bu La; its sodium content of 944 mg/100 g is driven by salt, soy sauce, and fish paste processing and cannot be reduced at the point of consumption — even a 50 g serving delivers approximately 472 mg sodium, nearly one-third of the recommended daily limit of 1500 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI tapioca starch base (GI ~75) with 35.4 g carbohydrate and 8.0 g sugar per 100 g, combined with only 0.9 g dietary fibre, produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Tian Bu La; the tapioca starch base has a high glycemic index (~75), dietary fibre is negligible at 0.9 g/100 g, and the 8.0 g sugar content further elevates glycaemic impact — a 50 g serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 13, which is clinically significant for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (944 mg/100 g), high total fat (12.84 g/100 g) from deep-frying, and ultra-processed formulation with estimated trans fat from repeated frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid Tian Bu La; the combination of very high sodium (944 mg/100 g), high fat from deep-frying (12.84 g/100 g), and ultra-processed fish paste formulation contributes to elevated blood pressure, arterial inflammation, and adverse lipid profiles — all established cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs); very high sodium worsens joint tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Tian Bu La; deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs, sodium at 944 mg/100 g exacerbates joint swelling, and the processed fish cake formulation provides no anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tian Bu La (甜不辣, also romanized as Tianbula) is a Taiwanese-origin deep-fried fish tempura product widely consumed as a hawker snack and street food in Singapore and Taiwan. The name is a phonetic transliteration of the Japanese word 'tempura' into Taiwanese Hokkien. It is made from a seasoned fish paste — typically using white fish such as pollock or lizardfish — combined with tapioca starch, sugar, salt, and soy sauce, then deep-fried until golden and slightly crispy on the outside while remaining chewy and dense inside. The texture is characteristically springy and bouncy, similar to fish cake but with a thicker, starchier coating. Tian Bu La is commonly served on skewers or sliced, often accompanied by sweet chilli sauce or a soy-based dipping sauce. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at 278 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 35.4 g/100 g dominated by tapioca starch (high GI ~75), moderate fat at 12.84 g/100 g from deep-frying, and relatively low protein at 5.3 g/100 g. Its sodium content of 944 mg/100 g is clinically significant and makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid retention. The high-GI tapioca starch base with minimal dietary fibre (0.9 g/100 g) and 8.0 g sugar/100 g makes it a poor choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Deep-frying also generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and relevant for rheumatoid arthritis management. Tian Bu La is classified as ultra-processed and is not suitable for health-conscious dietary patterns without strict portion control.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03060037) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on tapioca starch GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, documented as such. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Ontology anchors are defensible closest-parent matches with gap notes.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for Tian Bu La are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03060037 as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a sufficiently close match to provide these values with confidence. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca starch GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Tian Bu La exists in any approved GI source. Halal status is not confirmed — Tian Bu La may contain soy sauce with alcohol-based additives in some commercial preparations; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the manufacturer. The food is classified as ultra-processed due to fish paste processing, tapioca starch addition, and deep-frying. Ontology: foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' — no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 term exists for Tian Bu La; closest defensible parent nodes used with gap notes.
